title: Expected chances of death from non-communicable diseases, by gender
subtitle: The estimated share of 30-year-olds who would die before their 70th birthday from common non-communicable diseases: any of [cardiovascular disease](#dod:cvd), [cancer](#dod:cancer), [diabetes](#dod:diabetes), or [chronic respiratory disease](#dod:chronic_respiratory_disease).
note: Estimates assume that current mortality rates continue and they would not have already died from any other cause of death (e.g., injuries or [HIV/AIDS](#dod:hiv_aids).)
caption:
Expected chances of death from non-communicable diseases, by gender

The estimated share of 30-year-olds who would die before their 70th birthday from common non-communicable diseases: any of cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, or chronic respiratory disease.

This scatter plot visualizes the estimated share of 30-year-olds who would die before their 70th birthday from common non-communicable diseases in 2021, comparing probabilities for men on the vertical axis against women on the horizontal axis. The axes range from 0% to 50%, with grid lines marking 10% intervals. Data points are color-coded by continent, with North America in orange, South America in brown, Africa in purple, Europe in blue, Asia in teal, and Oceania in light blue. Circle sizes correspond to population, with a reference scale indicating circles for 2 billion and 7 billion people. Most countries cluster in the lower-left quadrant, where values for both genders fall below 20%. Japan sits near the bottom left with values around 5% for women and 10% for men. The United States, China, and Russia form a cluster in the mid-range, with women between 10% and 15% and men between 15% and 25%. Several African nations, including Lesotho, the Central African Republic, and Eritrea, appear in the upper-right section, showing values above 25% for women and exceeding 30% for men. Kiribati and the Solomon Islands represent outliers in the top right, with men’s probabilities approaching or exceeding 50%. The visual distribution shows a consistent pattern where men’s probabilities exceed women’s across nearly all labeled countries, as points sit above the diagonal line where x equals y. Estimates assume that current mortality rates continue and individuals would not have already died from any other cause of death, such as injuries or HIV/AIDS.
